$comment: Hand-authored BIM v0.1 for the Customer Onboarding reference scenario. Business-language only. See ADR-011 for the BIM vs AIM contract. This document is the source-of-truth for what business users want; it is intentionally partial and iterative, and the AI translation pipeline (BIM -> AIM -> CAM) resolves it into the deterministic canonical model.

metadata.name: Customer Onboarding
metadata.shortDescription: Let a bank onboard a new customer end to end, from the customer starting an application to a manager approving it.
metadata.primaryOutcome: A new customer exists in the bank's records, has been risk-assessed, and is ready to be issued products.

changeHistory.author: Dilip
changeHistory.summary: Initial hand-authored BIM for the first vertical slice.
changeHistory.rationale: Phase 0 reference artifact. Drives the shape of the BIM JSON Schema and the downstream AIM/CAM examples.

capability.statement: A prospective customer should be able to apply to become a customer of the bank. Bank staff should be able to check the application, ask for missing information, decide whether to accept it, and keep a full record of what happened and why.

included: Customers in India and the United States (Phase 1 launch countries)
Applications started by the customer themselves or by a branch officer on their behalf
Basic risk assessment based on customer type, country and declared income
A single approval step by a manager for high-risk cases
excluded: Product opening (accounts, cards, loans) — that is a separate capability
Sanctions and PEP screening beyond a placeholder check

successMetrics: Most low-risk applications are approved on the same working day.
No application can be approved without the required documents for its country and customer type.
Any decision (approve, reject, request more info) can be explained after the fact — which rules fired, what was seen, who acted.

personas.name: Applicant
personas.description: The person applying to become a customer, or a business's authorised representative applying on behalf of the business.
goals: Fill in the application without confusing jargon.
Only be asked for information that is actually needed for their situation.
Know what happens next and roughly when.

personas.name: Reviewer
personas.description: A bank operations analyst who checks applications for completeness and accuracy.
goals: Quickly see what changed since last time.
Ask the applicant for missing or better information without re-typing it.
Route the case to a manager if it is above their approval limit.

personas.name: Manager
personas.description: A senior operations manager who approves higher-risk applications.
goals: See the reviewer's notes and the risk assessment together.
Approve or reject with an audit-quality reason.
typicalActions: Send it back to the reviewer

personas.name: Auditor
personas.description: Read-only access for internal audit and compliance.
goals: See everything about any application at any point in time.
See who did what and why.

businessObjects.name: Customer
businessObjects.description: The person or business the bank is being asked to onboard.

attributes.name: Date of birth (individuals) or date of incorporation (businesses)
Tax identifier
attributes.note: Format and which one is required depends on country and customer type.

attributes.name: Declared annual income (individuals) or declared annual turnover (businesses)

businessObjects.name: Onboarding case
businessObjects.description: One attempt by an applicant to become a customer. Has a status, an owner, a history, and attached documents.

attributes.name: Case reference
attributes.note: Human-friendly identifier shown to the applicant and staff.

attributes.name: Status
attributes.note: Draft, submitted, in review, waiting for applicant, approved, rejected.

attributes.name: Risk score
attributes.note: Number from 0 to 10. Higher means more risk.

attributes.name: Risk band
attributes.note: Low, Medium, High. Derived from the score.

attributes.name: Decision reason
attributes.note: Free-text captured at approve or reject.

businessObjects.name: Document
businessObjects.description: A file the applicant uploads as part of their application.

attributes.name: Document type
attributes.note: e.g. Passport, PAN card, GST certificate, Utility bill.

workflow.description: A case moves through these stages. Most cases go through them in order but reviewers can send a case back for more information.

stages.name: Draft
stages.description: Applicant is still filling it in. Not visible to staff yet.

stages.name: Submitted
stages.description: Applicant has submitted. A reviewer is assigned automatically.

stages.name: In review
stages.description: A reviewer is actively working on it.

stages.name: Waiting for applicant
stages.description: Reviewer has asked for more information. Applicant can update and resubmit.

stages.name: Pending manager approval
stages.description: Reviewer has sent a high-risk case to a manager.

notes: Applicants can save a draft and come back to it for up to 30 days before it is auto-discarded.
Approved and rejected cases are read-only forever — no edits, only comments by staff with the right permission.

businessRules.statement: A tax identifier is always required, but which one depends on the country and customer type.
examples: An Indian individual must provide a PAN.
An Indian business must provide both a PAN and a GSTIN.
A US individual must provide an SSN or ITIN.
A US business must provide an EIN.

businessRules.statement: The set of required documents depends on the country and customer type.
examples: Indian individuals: PAN card image plus a proof of address.
Indian businesses: PAN card, GST certificate, and certificate of incorporation.
US individuals: Government-issued photo ID plus a proof of address.

businessRules.statement: If the risk score is above 7, a manager must approve the case. Otherwise the reviewer can approve it.
examples: A US individual with declared income under 20,000 USD and no proof of employment is high risk.
A business whose country of registration is different from its main operating country is high risk.

businessRules.statement: Some information is only visible to staff, not to the applicant.
examples: The internal risk score and risk band are visible to Reviewer, Manager and Auditor, but never to the Applicant.
The assigned reviewer's name is visible to Manager and Auditor only.

businessRules.statement: Any approval or rejection must include a reason. An empty reason is not allowed.

actions.description: Create a new draft onboarding case.
Attach a document of a given type to the case.
Send the completed application for review.
Ask the applicant for a specific missing or corrected piece of information.
Route the case to a manager because it is high risk or above the reviewer's authority.
Accept the application. A reason must be recorded. Managers can approve high-risk cases; reviewers only low and medium risk.
Reject the application with a documented reason.

documents.description: The set of document types the platform must be able to accept and label.
types: Proof of address (utility bill or bank statement)
documents.sizeLimitNote: Individual files should not be huge — a few megabytes each is fine for now.

nonFunctional.expectation: The application form should feel snappy on a normal broadband connection. No page should feel slow to load.
If something goes wrong mid-application, the applicant should not lose their work.
Only staff with the right role should be able to see internal fields like risk score. Uploaded documents must be stored safely.
Personal identifiers (tax IDs, dates of birth) must be treated as sensitive and only shown to those who need them.
Every decision must be explainable after the fact. Auditors should be able to answer 'why was this shown' and 'why did this rule fire' for any past case.
The applicant-facing form should be usable by people with common assistive technologies.

openQuestions.question: For Phase 0/1, is the risk score calculated by a simple rules table or by a scoring model? The example values assume a rules table.
How are reviewers assigned to submitted cases? Round-robin, workload-based, or manual pick from a queue?
Draft retention is assumed to be 30 days. Is that the actual policy?
